en.extract:
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher (Nephelomyias ochraceiventris) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Bolivia and Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher was originally described in 1873 as Mitrephorus ochraceiventris. From the early twentieth century it was classified in genus Myiophobus. A study published in 2009 determined that the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher and two other species did not belong in Myiophobus so the genus Nephelomyias was created for them in 2010.
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is monotypic. It and the orange-banded flycatcher (N. lintoni) are sister species and form a superspecies.


Description
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is 12 to 14 cm (4.7 to 5.5 in) long and weighs 8.2 to 13 g (0.29 to 0.46 oz). The sexes are very similar. Adult males have an olive crown with a partly hidden orange (in the north) to red-orange (in the south) patch in the middle. Both sexes have dusky lores and a thin buffy eye-ring on an otherwise yellowish olive face. Their back and rump are olive to olive brown. Their wings are dusky with wide whitish or buffy tips on the wing coverts that show as two wing bars. Their tail is dusky. Their throat and breast are ochraceous yellow to ochraceous buff and their belly yellow. Adult females have a smaller, reddish chestnut, crown patch. Adults have a dark brown iris, black legs and feet, and a black bill. Juveniles usually do not have a crown patch; if present it is yellow in males.


Distribution and habitat
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is found on the eastern slope of the Andes from central Amazonas Department in northern Peru south into Bolivia as far as eastern La Paz Department. It inhabits the interior and edges of humid montane forest and elfin forest. In elevation it ranges between 2,200 and 3,700 m (7,200 and 12,100 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is believed to be a year-round resident.


Feeding
The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is believed to feed mostly on insects and some small fruits. It typically forages in small groups (possibly families) from the forest's mid-story to its canopy and often joins mixed-species feeding flocks. It often perches upright on large heavy leaves. It takes prey from foliage with short flights to hover-glean.


Breeding
Only one nest of the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is known. It was a globe built into moss and ferns 60 cm (2 ft) above the ground. Discovered in October, it contained two eggs that were white with a few brown speckles. The incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.


Vocalization
As of early 2025 xeno-canto had only six recordings of ochraceous-breasted flycatcher vocalizations; the Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Macaulay Library had 12 with some overlap. The species' song is "a chip followed by a shrill, short trill: tsip...tsop-teeerrr" and its calls are "sharp tsip or tip notes".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher as being of Least Concern. Its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common in Peru. "Human activity probably has little short term effect on Ochraceous-breasted Flycatcher."


References

The '''ochraceous-breasted flycatcher''' ('''''Nephelomyias ochraceiventris''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in [[Bolivia]] and [[Peru]].<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher was [[Species description|originally described]] in 1873 as ''Mitrephorus ochraceiventris''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Cabanis |first=Jean Louis | journal=Journal für Ornithologie | title=Protokoll der LV. Monats-Sitzung | volume=21 | page=319 | publisher=Friedländer | date= 1873 | language=German |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32696867 | access-date=January 16, 2025 }}</ref> From the early twentieth century it was classified in genus ''[[Myiophobus]]''. A study published in 2009 determined that the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher and two other species did not belong in ''Myiophobus'' so the genus ''Nephelomyias'' was created for them in 2010.<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 18 November 2024. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved November 26, 2024</ref><ref>Ohlson, J., Fjeldså, J. & Ericson, P. G. P. 2009. A new genus for three species of tyrant flycatchers (Passeriformes: Tyrannidae), formerly placed in ''Myiophobus''. Zootaxa 2290: 36-40.</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCRecentChanges.htm |title=Recognize ''Nephelomyias'' Ohlson et al., 2009 (Proposal 425) |last=Remsen |first=J. V. Jr. |date=August 10, 2010 |website=Recent Changes |publisher=South American Classification Committee |access-date=January 15, 2025 }}</ref>

The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/> It and the [[orange-banded flycatcher]] (''N. lintoni'') are [[sister species]] and form a [[superspecies]].<ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 18 November 2024. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved November 26, 2024</ref>

==Description==

The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is {{convert|12|to|14|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|8.2|to|13|g|oz|abbr=on}}. The sexes are very similar. Adult males have an olive crown with a partly hidden orange (in the north) to red-orange (in the south) patch in the middle. Both sexes have dusky [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and a thin buffy [[eye-ring]] on an otherwise yellowish olive face. Their back and rump are olive to olive brown. Their wings are dusky with wide whitish or buffy tips on the wing [[coverts]] that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their tail is dusky. Their throat and breast are ochraceous yellow to ochraceous buff and their belly yellow. Adult females have a smaller, reddish chestnut, crown patch. Adults have a dark brown iris, black legs and feet, and a black bill. Juveniles usually do not have a crown patch; if present it is yellow in males.<ref name=OBFL-BOW>Schulenberg, T. S. and T. Johnson (2020). Ochraceous-breasted Flycatcher (''Nephelomyias ochraceiventris''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.ocbfly2.01 retrieved January 16, 2025</ref><ref name=Schulenberg>{{cite book | last =Schulenberg | first =T.S. | last2 =Stotz | first2 =D.F. | last3 =Lane | first3 =D.F. | last4 =O'Neill | first4 =J.P. | last5 =Parker | first5 =T.A. III | title =Birds of Peru | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =revised and updated |series=Princeton Field Guides | date =2010 | location =Princeton, NJ | page =444  |isbn = 978-0-691-13023-1 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is found on the eastern slope of the Andes from central [[Amazonas (Peruvian department)|Amazonas Department]] in northern Peru south into Bolivia as far as eastern [[La Paz Department (Bolivia)|La Paz Department]]. It inhabits the interior and edges of humid [[montane forest]] and [[elfin forest]]. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|2200|and|3700|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=OBFL-BOW/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is believed to be a year-round resident.<ref name=OBFL-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is believed to feed mostly on insects and some small fruits. It typically forages in small groups (possibly families) from the forest's mid-story to its canopy and often joins [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s. It often perches upright on large heavy leaves. It takes prey from foliage with short flights to hover-glean.<ref name=OBFL-BOW/><ref name=Schulenberg/>

===Breeding===

Only one nest of the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher is known. It was a globe built into moss and ferns {{convert|60|cm|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground. Discovered in October, it contained two eggs that were white with a few brown speckles. The incubation period, time to fledging, and details of parental care are not known.<ref name=OBFL-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Nephelomyias-ochraceiventris |species=the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher}}
===Vocalization===

As of early 2025 [[xeno-canto]] had only six recordings of ochraceous-breasted flycatcher vocalizations; the [[Cornell Lab of Ornithology]]'s [[Macaulay Library]] had 12 with some overlap. The species' song is "a chip followed by a shrill, short trill: ''tsip...tsop-teeerrr''" and its calls are "sharp ''tsip'' or ''tip'' notes".<ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the ochraceous-breasted flycatcher as being of Least Concern. Its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common in Peru.<ref name=Schulenberg/> "Human activity probably has little short term effect on Ochraceous-breasted Flycatcher."<ref name=OBFL-BOW/>
